tasks.title: Split require_repoprompt config into plan_required and tool_injection flags with iteration-aware behavior
evidence: crates/ralph/src/contracts.rs:108: require_repoprompt is a single Option<bool> field in AgentConfig.
docs/configuration.md:28: require_repoprompt is documented as a single boolean flag.
crates/ralph/src/run_cmd.rs:286-334: Multiple iterations are handled in a loop, with is_followup = iteration_index > 1.
crates/ralph/src/promptflow.rs:92-112: build_phase1_prompt receives policy.require_repoprompt and passes it to render_worker_phase1_prompt.
User request: Separate into 'repoprompt_plan_required' (injects context_builder planning instructions) and 'repoprompt_tool_injection' (only injects tooling reminders).

User request: When multiple iterations are enabled, do NOT honor 'repoprompt_plan_required' config for phase 1 of subsequent iterations (only include it in the first iteration). DO honor 'repoprompt_tool_injection' config for all iterations.
plan: Add two new fields to AgentConfig in contracts.rs: repoprompt_plan_required (Option<bool>) and repoprompt_tool_injection (Option<bool>).
Update AgentConfig::merge_from to merge the new fields.
Update Config::default() in contracts.rs to set appropriate defaults for both new fields.
Update the schema (schemas/config.schema.json) to include the two new fields.
Update PromptPolicy in promptflow.rs to have separate fields: repoprompt_plan_required and repoprompt_tool_injection.
Update run_cmd.rs to create PromptPolicy with both flags, checking iteration context (is_followup) when building phase 1 prompts.

Modify build_phase1_prompt in promptflow.rs to accept iteration context (is_followup) and conditionally exclude repoprompt_plan_required for followup iterations while always including repoprompt_tool_injection.
Update execute_phase1_planning in phases.rs to pass is_followup context to build_phase1_prompt.

Find all other usages of require_repoprompt in promptflow.rs and run_cmd/phases.rs and update logic to use the new flags appropriately (repoprompt_tool_injection for all phases, repoprompt_plan_required only for first iteration phase 1).
Add tests covering: both flags true, only plan_required true, only tool_injection true, both false, and iteration-specific behavior (plan_required excluded in followup iterations).
Update docs/configuration.md to document the new separate flags and iteration-specific behavior.
Deprecate but keep require_repoprompt for backward compatibility, mapping it to repoprompt_plan_required for migration.
tasks.request: Separate the repoprompt config settings into 2 configuration settings instead of 1. One config setting for 'repoprompt_plan_required' and one for 'repoprompt_tool_injection'. The first config injects the context_builder planning instructions while the latter only injects the repo prompt tooling reminders. When multiple iterations are enabled, do NOT honor the 'repoprompt_plan_required' config for phase 1 of the subsequent iterations. Only include it in the first iteration. DO honor the 'repoprompt_tool_injection' config for all iterations. Phase 1 of the subsequent iterations will still plan, just don't include the mandatory instructions to use the repo prompt context_builder.

tasks.request: Add a capability to do multiple iterations per task. You may ask, why? The reason is that some models are more capable than others. Oftentimes, a dumber model does syntactically correct and functional code, but it does it in a way that is sloppy. So by having that same dumber model run across the same task multiple times, it enables iteration that enhances outcomes. On the initial loop, the agents have to examine the current state, and then come up with the solution and implement the solution for the task at hand. This leads to a lot of context being used on exploration and contemplation. Context gets bloated, and quality decreases because a lot of irrelevant information ends up in the context. On the subsequent passes for the same task, since the task has already been achieved, it has more of a clear context to be able to focus on how to do it better instead of having to come up with the new implementation solution altogether. Iteration enhances outcomes and leads to better outcomes. So for example, keep the three-phased approach, but do two loops on the same task, on the second go-round, it will be prompted that it had already been executed once before, and on the second go-round, it makes sure to identify any unexpected or unplanned behavior changes, poor coding habits. Outstanding follow-ups that need to be executed. And just does overall refinement to make it a better approach. Think of it like multiple senior developers doing like a peer review refinement process per task. This will lead to higher outcomes. Currently, the three-phase approach consists of phase one planning, phase two implementing the plan, and phase three completing any outstanding work that remained after phase two, as well as doing a thorough review for bugs, regressions, etc. Phase three is essentially like a code review, but it can also make changes. We will likely want to tweak the prompts for the second loop, or what. I want to avoid using any verbiage in the second and any subsequent loops that denotes that it is doing a second pass or a third pass, etc., because that may cause the agent to get a little lazy and assume that things are already done. I want to encourage the comprehensive approach liDon't repeat yourself as certainly a principle I want in place. Code readability, code maintainability, concise code, elegantly simple code but not feature or functionally simple code. I think it would be beneficial to be able to have different levels of reasoning applied for the initial pass of the task and the subsequent passes. I could see some potential downsides, but the reason to do this is to fold one to decrease runtime and for cost purposes, but if we have a model run on the first pass with high reasoning, it can spend a lot of time on doing it correctly or mostly correct on that initial pass and then on subsequent runs, since the task is overall a bit easier due to existing solutions being in place, we could have a lower reasoning level configured. We already allow for the reasoning effort to be controlled globally and by project in the config file, as well as have task-specific reasoning set in the task queue.

tasks.request: Add support for a 'model_effort' field in the queue schema. This field defaults to a value of 'default'. If the runner and model executing the task (currently only codex runner) supports a reasoning effort level the value of 'model_effort' overrides the global and repo config value of reasoning_effort when executing this task. A value of 'default', or if 'model_effort' is excluded, uses the value provided by the config. This enables users to specify variable reasoning efforts based on the task. The task builder should prefer to lean on 'default' but it may choose to set the value to any of the following (all of which are supported by codex, the only runner supporting this at the moment): low, medium (codex default), high, xhigh. If our accepted reasoning levels in ralph differ from these then update them accordingly to match.
